clinical_coherence.notes: 19M (male -- atypical demographics for NMDAR) college freshman presenting with acute psychosis, brought by campus police. The male sex, police involvement, and college setting all create cognitive biases toward substance-induced psychosis or first-break schizophrenia. However, the clinical features -- orofacial dyskinesias, dystonic posturing, waxy flexibility (catatonia), autonomic instability, and seizures -- form a complete NMDAR phenotype. Family autoimmune history (Hashimoto's in mother, celiac in sister) provides subtle predisposition clues.

tool_output_consistency.notes: CSF shows lymphocytic pleocytosis (45 WBC), oligoclonal bands, negative HSV PCR, NMDAR antibodies pending then positive at 1:128. EEG with extreme delta brush and bilateral temporal seizures. MRI with subtle bilateral temporal FLAIR changes. Labs with negative drug screen, elevated CK. Testicular ultrasound reveals teratoma. All outputs perfectly concordant.

ground_truth_quality.notes: ICD G04.81 correct. The diagnosis of NMDAR encephalitis with testicular teratoma in a male is clinically accurate. Critical actions correctly include testicular ultrasound screening and orchiectomy. The teaching that ~20% of NMDAR cases are male is essential.

difficulty_calibration.notes: Outstanding puzzle calibration. Male sex (atypical), campus police involvement (priming toward substance use), college student (first-break psychosis age), and negative drug screen all create multiple layers of diagnostic challenge. The testicular teratoma as the trigger in a male is a rarely discussed but important clinical entity.

demographic_plausibility.notes: 19M Caucasian college freshman is realistic. The rubric specifies P02 should be male, and this case delivers. Male NMDAR encephalitis accounts for ~20% of cases. Family autoimmune predisposition (Hashimoto's, celiac) is well-established.

red_herring_quality.notes: Three excellent red herrings: male sex (may not consider NMDAR), campus police involvement (primes toward substance-induced psychosis), and subtle temporal FLAIR changes (may suggest HSV). Each is a realistic cognitive bias with clear correct interpretation.

educational_value.notes: Exceptional teaching case: NMDAR encephalitis in males, testicular teratoma screening, the danger of dismissing organic psychosis as substance-induced, and the importance of negative drug screen redirecting the differential. The psychiatric consultation note correctly identifying red flags is an excellent teaching tool. The follow-up outputs (testicular US, CT staging, tumor markers, psych consult) are outstanding.

strengths: Male patient with testicular teratoma addresses an important knowledge gap
Red herrings (male sex, campus police, temporal FLAIR) are clever and realistic
Psychiatric consultation note correctly identifying organic red flags is an excellent teaching tool
Negative drug screen as a pivotal redirecting finding
Comprehensive follow-up outputs including testicular US and tumor markers
Family autoimmune history providing subtle predisposition clues
